advice: Stay on well-lit main roads where possible. Avoid shortcuts through alleys or parks.
why: Well-lit and busier routes reduce opportunity for crime and increase the chance of bystander intervention.
source: UK Home Office – Personal Safety Advice

advice: Cross the road or change direction. Head toward a busy shop or public place. Call 999 if the threat feels real.
why: Changing your path tests whether someone is genuinely following. Moving toward people makes you a harder target.
source: Suzy Lamplugh Trust – Personal Safety Tips

advice: Tell a trusted contact your location before your phone dies. Note landmarks around you.
why: Sharing your location means someone knows where to start looking if you lose contact.

advice: Stand in a well-lit area near other people. Keep your belongings close and stay alert.
why: Attackers look for isolated distracted targets. Visibility and awareness deter approach.

advice: Share your trip details with a trusted contact. Check the driver's name and number plate before getting in.
why: Ride-share impersonation is a known risk. Verifying the vehicle prevents getting into the wrong car.

advice: Keep the volume low enough to hear your surroundings or use only one earbud.
why: Full noise isolation removes your ability to detect approaching footsteps or vehicles.
source: Suzy Lamplugh Trust – Personal Safety Tips

advice: Have your keys ready before reaching your door. Check your surroundings before stopping.
why: Fumbling for keys while stationary makes you vulnerable. Being prepared minimises the time you are a still target.

advice: Park near exits and in well-lit bays. Scan the area as you approach your vehicle.
why: Car parks with poor lighting are common locations for theft and assault. Proximity to exits means a quicker escape if needed.
source: Secured by Design – Car Park Safety

advice: Trust your instincts. If something feels wrong turn around or take a different route.
why: Research shows that gut feelings about danger are often accurate subconscious threat assessments.
source: Gavin de Becker – The Gift of Fear (safety principle)

advice: Move away calmly and find other people. You do not owe politeness to someone who makes you feel unsafe.
why: Social pressure to be polite is the number one tool used by predatory individuals to maintain access.

advice: Keep your phone in an inside pocket or hold it firmly with both hands. Avoid using it while standing still on a kerb.
why: Phone snatching on foot and by moped is most common when victims are distracted at the roadside.

advice: Tell someone your route and expected return time. Vary your routine so it is not predictable.
why: Predictable patterns make it easier for someone with bad intent to plan an encounter.

advice: Do not engage or make eye contact. Cross the road calmly and keep moving at a steady pace.
why: Engagement — even a look — can be interpreted as a challenge. Creating distance quietly is the safest de-escalation.

advice: Use ATMs inside banks or shops when possible. Shield your PIN and be aware of who is nearby.
why: Outdoor ATMs are common sites for shoulder surfing and distraction theft.

advice: Never leave your drink unattended. Accept drinks only from bar staff directly.
why: Drink spiking remains a significant risk in nightlife settings. Keeping control of your drink eliminates the main vector.

situation: Meeting someone from online for the first time
advice: Always meet in a busy public place for the first few meetings. Tell a friend where you are going and when you expect to be back.
why: Meeting in public means bystanders are present and escape routes are available. Letting someone know your plans creates accountability.

situation: Feeling pressured at a social event
advice: Have an exit plan before you arrive. Agree a code word with a friend so they can help you leave without awkwardness.
why: Pre-planning removes the social pressure of making a decision in the moment. A code word lets a friend extract you discreetly.

advice: Block the person immediately and save screenshots as evidence. Report the behaviour to the platform and to police if it escalates.
why: Blocking stops contact and preserves your wellbeing. Evidence is required if you need to apply for a restraining order or report harassment.

situation: Being filmed or photographed without your consent
advice: Ask the person to delete the image calmly but firmly. If they refuse or share it, it may be a criminal offence — report it to police.
why: Non-consensual filming in public or private is covered by the Online Safety Act 2023 in the UK. You have the right to report it.

advice: Move toward a group of people or enter a shop. Speak loudly and clearly if you need help. Report the incident to police after.
why: Being vocal in public alerts bystanders and can deter a harasser. Reporting creates a record that supports future action.

situation: Peer pressure to do something that feels unsafe
advice: You have the right to say no without explanation. A simple firm statement is more effective than a lengthy excuse.
why: Research shows that giving reasons for a refusal invites counter-arguments. A direct no is legally and socially valid.

situation: Sharing personal details at social events
advice: Avoid sharing your home address or daily routine with people you have just met. Use a first name only if you are unsure.
why: Oversharing location and routine information with strangers creates a vulnerability that can be exploited later.
source: National Crime Prevention Council – Personal Information

situation: Forcibly taken or grabbed against your will
advice: Make as much noise as possible immediately — scream and attract attention. Drop your weight to the ground to make yourself harder to move. Fight back if it is safe to do so and run toward people or lights. Call 999 as soon as you can.
why: Resistance in the first moments is your strongest defence. Kidnappers rely on speed and compliance — noise and dead weight significantly reduce their ability to move you. Studies show victims who resist in the first 30 seconds are far more likely to escape.
source: National Crime Agency – Personal Safety / Suzy Lamplugh Trust

advice: Stay on populated routes and vary your walking times. Carry your phone charged.
why: Dog walkers can be targeted because their routine is predictable and they are often in quieter areas.

advice: Avoid logging into banking or email on public networks. Use mobile data for sensitive tasks.
why: Public Wi-Fi can be intercepted to steal login credentials and personal data.

advice: Use a trusted app to share your live location with at least one contact when travelling.
why: Real-time location sharing means help can find you quickly if you stop responding.

advice: If a situation feels wrong leave immediately. You can always apologise later — you cannot undo harm.
why: Multiple safety organisations agree: your subconscious processes threats faster than conscious analysis. Act on discomfort.
source: Suzy Lamplugh Trust – Trust Your Instincts

advice: Check the route in daylight first if possible. Note safe spots such as shops and busy junctions along the way.
why: Familiarity with a route reduces anxiety and increases your ability to react to unexpected situations.

situation: Feeling panicked or overwhelmed in an unsafe situation
advice: Take a slow breath in for 4 counts and out for 4. Focus on one action at a time — move away from the threat if you can. Call 999 if you are in immediate danger. You do not have to speak — stay on the line and they will trace you.
why: Panic narrows your thinking and makes decisions harder. Slow breathing activates your body's calming system and restores clear thought. Emergency operators are trained to help even if you cannot speak.
source: Mental Health Foundation – Crisis Grounding / College of Policing

situation: Lost and unsure of your surroundings
advice: Stop walking and stay in a well-lit public place. Open your maps app or ask a member of staff in a nearby shop. Share your live location with a trusted contact until you reach somewhere safe.
why: Moving while disoriented can take you further from safety. Stopping lets you gather information calmly. Shops and cafes are generally safe places to ask for directions or wait.

advice: Do not retaliate physically — this escalates the situation. Walk away calmly and tell a trusted adult as soon as possible. Write down what happened with dates and details. Schools are legally required to have an anti-bullying policy.
why: Physical retaliation rarely ends bullying and often results in the victim being punished too. Having a written record enables schools and authorities to take formal action.

advice: Screenshot and save all evidence before blocking the person. Report the content to the platform. If it involves threats or sexual images tell a trusted adult and report to police. Contact Childline on 0800 1111 — it is free and confidential.
why: Platforms must legally act on reports of abusive content. Saving evidence first means you have proof even after the post is deleted. You do not have to handle this alone.

situation: Not knowing what to do or who to call when unsafe
advice: In immediate danger: call 999. Non-urgent police: call 101. Emotional support any time: Samaritans 116 123 (free). Under 19 and worried: Childline 0800 1111 (free). If you cannot speak to 999 press 55 on your keypad to signal you need help.
why: Having a clear list removes the paralysis of not knowing who to contact. The 55 silent signal allows police to confirm a real emergency without a voice call.
source: Samaritans / Metropolitan Police – Who to Call

situation: Feeling watched or like something is wrong
advice: Trust that feeling immediately. Remove yourself from the situation without hesitation — enter a shop or public building and stay near staff or other people. You do not need to justify leaving to anyone.
why: Your subconscious detects threat cues faster than conscious reasoning. Acting on discomfort is always the right choice. You can apologise for leaving later — you cannot undo harm.
source: Gavin de Becker – The Gift of Fear / Suzy Lamplugh Trust
